---
title: Pipeline for Fusarium spp Whole Genome Sequencing Analysis on Ceres and Atlas
  HPC clusters
author: "Maxwell Chibuogwu"
date: "2025-06-09"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Introduction

This document outlines a comprehensive bioinformatics pipeline for the analysis of Fusarium whole-genome sequencing data generated by Oxford Nanopore Technologies. The workflow covers initial quality control, genome assembly, polishing, evaluation, and functional annotation. The commands are tailored for a High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment that uses the Slurm workload manager i.e., SciNet's Ceres and Atlas).

# Key Stages of the Pipeline

1. Initial Setup: Environment and data preparation.
2. QC and Filtering: Read dereplication, adapter trimming, and quality filtering.
3. Genome Assembly: Assembling long reads into contigs using Flye or wtdbg2.
4. Assembly Polishing: Error correction of the draft assembly.
5. Assembly Evaluation: Assessing assembly completeness with BUSCO.
6. Genome Annotation: Identifying repeats and predicting genes with Funannotate.

## i. Basecalling POD5 files

This section covers the commands for basecalling on *Atlas*.

Navigate to the project's working directory containing the pod5 files on the 90-day storage.
Save this code chunk as a submit file e.g., basecall_pod5.sh and submit using "sbatch basecall_pod5.sh"

```{bash, Basecalling}
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name="Dor_Basecall"          # name of this job
#SBATCH -A silage_microbiome               # account
#SBATCH -p gpu-a100                        # partition (queue)
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:1                  # request 1 A100 GPU
#SBATCH --qos=normal                       # QoS
#SBATCH -n 40                              # number of logical cores
#SBATCH -t 5:00:00                         # time (5 hrs)
#SBATCH --array=36-45                      # job array: barcodes 36 through 45
#SBATCH --mail-user=maxwell.chibuogwu@usda.gov
#SBATCH --mail-type=BEGIN,END,FAIL
#SBATCH -o "stdout.%A_%a.%N"               # %A = job ID, %a = array index
#SBATCH -e "stderr.%A_%a.%N"

# Load modules
module purge
module load dorado

date

# Each array task gets its own barcode number
BARCODE=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

# Run dorado basecaller on the corresponding barcode folder
dorado basecaller sup barcode${BARCODE} -r \
  --device cuda:all \
  --kit-name SQK-NBD114-96 \
  --min-qscore 15 \
  > bar${BARCODE}_pod5s.bam

date

dorado summary bar${BARCODE}_pod5s.bam > basecall_bar${BARCODE}_summary_stats.tsv

module purge

```

The end of the previous operation should output several ".bam" files containing used barcodes other barcodes and unclassified barcodes.
Copy the needed/used barcodes' bam files to a new directory and use Samtools to convert the bam files to zipped fastq using the script below.
COntained in the directory should be sample/barcode names listed in `barlist.txt` i.e., barcodeXX 

Save this code chunk as a submit file e.g., demuxed_bam_to_fastq.sh and submit using "sbatch demuxed_bam_to_fastq.sh"
```{bash, Converting Bam to fastq}
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH --job-name="Demultiplex"   	   #name of this job
#SBATCH -A silage_microbiome		   #name of account
#SBATCH -p atlas
#SBATCH --qos=normal		 	   #Quality of service for the job
#SBATCH -n 40                   	   #number of logical cores
#SBATCH -t 24:00:00        		   #time allocated for this job hours:mins:seconds
#SBATCH --mail-user=maxwell.chibuogwu@usda.gov   #enter your email address to receive emails
#SBATCH --mail-type=BEGIN,END,FAIL #will receive an email when job starts, ends or fails
#SBATCH -o "stdout.%j.%N"          # standard output, %j adds job number to output file name and %N adds the node name
#SBATCH -e "stderr.%j.%N"          #optional, prints standard error

date
module purge

module load samtools
while read in; do samtools fastq *${in}.bam | gzip > ${in}.fastq.gz; done < barlist.txt

module purge
date
```

## 1. Initial Setup and Environment Preparation

This section covers the initial setup, including creating a dedicated conda environment and preparing the workspace on Ceres

```{bash}
# Navigate to the project's working directory on the 90-day storage.
# It's good practice to work in 90daydata space for active computation and 'unlimited' storage.
cd /90daydata/silage_microbiome/max_seq/Max_pod_5s/isolate_fastq/

# Create a dedicated conda environment to manage all bioinformatics tools and dependencies.
# This ensures reproducibility and avoids conflicts between package versions.
salloc # Obtain a slurm job default allocation to avoid loading modules or performing computation on the login node
conda create --prefix /project/silage_microbiome/max.chi/seqenv

# Activate the newly created environment.
conda activate /project/silage_microbiome/max.chi/seqenv

# Install necessary tools into the environment.
# It's best to install them in one go if possible, specifying channels with -c.
conda install -c bioconda seqkit nanofilt porechop
```


## 2. Pre-processing: Quality Control and Filtering

This stage cleans the raw FASTQ files to improve the quality of the downstream assembly. It involves removing duplicate reads, trimming adapters, and filtering by read length.

### Step 2.1: Consolidate Barcoded Reads

If reads are separated by barcode into different folders, a first step is to combine them.

```{bash}
# This command concatenates all FASTQ files within a barcode's folder into a single file.
# --wrap='' allows sbatch to execute the string as a shell command.
sbatch -A silage_microbiome -N 1 -n 1 --mem=10000 -p ceres -t 1-0 --wrap='cat *.fastq.gz > barcodeX.fastq.gz'
```

### Step 2.2: Batch Processing Setup

```{bash}
# Create a file named 'barlist.txt' that contains the base name of each sample, one per line.
# For example:
# barcode49
# barcode50
# barcode51
# ...etc
nano barlist.txt
```


### Step 2.3 Dereplication, Adapter Trimming, and Filtering

The following commands are wrapped in `while` loops to efficiently process all samples listed in `barlist.txt`.

```{bash}
# Activate the conda environment if not already active.
module load miniconda
source activate /project/silage_microbiome/max.chi/seqenv

### BATCH 1: Trim adapters using Porechop ###
# Crockpot the pore chop command to search and remove Oxford Nanopore Adapter Sequences and output a fastq file 
# Porechop requires a different python environment, so we unload miniconda.
module unload miniconda
module load porechop
# -i: Input FASTQ file.
# -o: Output FASTQ file.
# -t 70: Number of threads to use.

sbatch -A silage_microbiome --array=1-10 -N 1 -n 16 --mem=50000 -p ceres -t 1-00 --wrap='in=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" barlist.txt); porechop-runner.py -i 01_QC/${in}.fastq.gz -o 03_Trimmed_Data/PC_${in}.fastq -t 16'
# Unload porechop to avoid python conflicts later.
module unload porechop

### BATCH 2: Remove duplicate reads using seqkit rmdup ###
# -n: checks by sequence name to find duplicates.
# -o: specifies the output file for dereplicated sequences.
# -D: specifies the output file for the list of duplicated reads.
sbatch -A silage_microbiome -N 1 -n 4 --mem=300000 -p ceres --wrap='while read in; do seqkit rmdup ${in}.fastq -n -o ${in}_D.fastq -D ${in}_derep_list.txt; done < barlist.txt'


### BATCH 3: Filter reads by length using NanoFilt ###
# Activate our main conda environment again.
module load miniconda
source activate /project/silage_microbiome/max.chi/seqenv
# -l 1000: Filters out any reads shorter than 1000 base pairs.
# -q 20: Filters out any reads with phred q score greater than 20
# This command reads from the deduplicated (from seqkit) fastq
while read in; do NanoFilt -l 500 PC_${in}.fastq > ${in}_filt.fastq; done < barlist.txt

#Used to improve filtration and quality
while read in; do NanoFilt -q 20 -l 1000 03_Trimmed_Data/${in}_D.fastq > 03_Trimmed_Data/1k_filt/${in}_filt.fastq; done < barlist.txt

```

### Step 2.4: Quality Assessment with NanoPlot

After filtering, it's crucial to assess the quality of the reads.

```{bash}
# This loop creates a separate directory for each sample's NanoPlot report.
while read in; do mkdir ${in}_nanoplot; done < barlist.txt

# Run NanoPlot on the final filtered FASTQ files.
# --fastq: Input FASTQ file.
# --N50: Include N50 statistics in the report.
# -o: Output directory for the report.
# -t: Number of threads.
sbatch -A silage_microbiome --array=1-10 -N 1 -n 16 --mem=50000 -p ceres -t 1:00:00 --wrap='in=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" barlist.txt); NanoPlot --fastq 03_Trimmed_Data/500/${in}_filt.fastq --N50 -o 04_Summary_Plots/500/${in}_nanoplot -t 16'
```


## 3. Genome Assembly

Assembly with Flye
```{bash}
# Load the Flye module if it's not in the conda environment.
module load flye

# Command to run Flye for a single sample (e.g., barcode56).
# --nano-corr: Specifies using nanopore reads with pre-correction.
# --threads: Number of threads.
# --genome-size: Estimated genome size (e.g., 50m for 50 megabases). Essential for good results.
# --out-dir: Output directory for the assembly.
sbatch -A silage_microbiome -N 1 -n 40 --mem=50000 -p ceres -t 1-0 --wrap='flye --nano-corr barcode36_filt.fastq --threads 40 --genome-size 50m --asm-coverage 100 --iterations 1 --out-dir barcode56_flye_assembly'

#Since batch operation does not work, repeat assembly for all barcodes 
```

## 4. Assembly Polishing on Atlas

Polishing corrects errors in the draft assembly by mapping the raw reads back to the assembled contigs and generating a new consensus. This pipeline uses `minimap2` and `wtpoa-cns`.

```{bash, Polishing}
# Ensure required modules are loaded
module load miniconda
source activate /project/silage_microbiome/max.chi/seqenv # minimap2 and samtools should be here
```
# Save this code chunk as a submit file e.g., "assem_polish_all.sh" and submit using "sbatch assem_polish_all.sh"
```{bash}
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name="polish_assembly"        # Job name
#SBATCH --account=silage_microbiome         # Account
#SBATCH -p gpu-a100                        #name of the partition (queue) you are submitting to
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:1
#SBATCH --qos=normal                       #Quality of service for the job
#SBATCH -n 16                              #number of logical cores
#SBATCH -t 10:00:00                         # Walltime (hh:mm:ss)
#SBATCH --array=36-45                       # Run for barcodes 36 through 45
#SBATCH -o "polish_log/stdout.%A_%a.%N"            # Stdout (%A = job ID, %a = array index)
#SBATCH -e "polish_log/stderr.%A_%a.%N"            # Stderr

# Load required modules
module purge
module load dorado
module load samtools

date

# Each array task gets its own barcode number
BARCODE=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

# Input files (adjust paths if needed)
ASSEMBLY="bar${BARCODE}_assembly.fasta"
READS="bar${BARCODE}_pod5s.bam"

# Output files
ALIGNED="aligned_bar${BARCODE}_calls.bam"
POLISHED="polished${BARCODE}_assembly.fasta"

# Align reads to assembly
dorado aligner "${ASSEMBLY}" "${READS}" | \
    samtools sort --threads 40 > "${ALIGNED}"

# Index BAM
samtools index "${ALIGNED}"

# Detect the correct RG ID for this barcode
RG=$(samtools view -H "${ALIGNED}" | grep "^@RG" | grep "barcode${BARCODE}" | \
     awk '{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++) if($i ~ /^ID:/) print substr($i,4)}')
date
# Polish assembly using the detected RG
dorado polish --RG "${RG}" "${ALIGNED}" "${ASSEMBLY}" > "${POLISHED}"

date
module purge
```


```{bash, tricks on Atlas}
# Salloc
salloc -A silage_microbiome -p gpu-a100 -n 2 --gres=gpu:a100:1 -t 20:00:00
# Start interactive session
srun -A silage_microbiome --pty -n 16 -N 1 --mem=10G bash
# Submit job script
sbatch submit.sh
# Check active jobs
squeue -all --me
# Check Job Statistics
sstat jobID --all
# Check accounts
sacctmgr show associations where user=$USER format=account%20,qos%50

#Lifesaver to restart 90daydata storage files
find /90daydata/silage_microbiome -type f -exec touch {} +
```


## 5. Assembly Evaluation with BUSCO
BUSCO (Benchmarking Universal Single-Copy Orthologs) assesses the completeness of a genome assembly by checking for the presence of a curated set of expected genes.

```{bash}
# Run BUSCO on the polished assembly.
# -i: Input FASTA file.
# -o: Output directory name.
# -l: Lineage dataset to use (e.g., hypocreales for Fusarium).
# --mode: Type of analysis (genome, transcriptome, proteins).
# -c: Number of cores/threads.
sbatch -A silage_microbiome -N 1 -n 4 --mem=40000 -p ceres -t 1:00:00 --wrap='busco -i polished40_assembly.fasta -o polished40_assembly.fasta_busco_eval -l hypocreales --mode genome -c 40'
#The above line would make it possible to run busco offline - avoiding redownloads of 
#For batch running after downloading the busco files (busco_downloads) based on one download, run offline as below where array is the range of number of polished files in XX.txt 
sbatch -A silage_microbiome --array=1-9 -N 1 -n 4 --mem=40000 -p ceres -t 1:00:00 --wrap='in=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" pol_barlist.txt); busco -i ${in} -o ${in}_busco_eval -l hypocreales --mode genome -c 40 --offline'

# The key output is the short summary text file, which gives percentages for Complete, Fragmented, and Missing BUSCO genes.
```


## 6. Genome Annotation with Funannotate

Funannotate is a pipeline for annotating eukaryotic genomes. It automates masking, gene prediction, and functional annotation.

**Prerequisites: Environment Setup**
Annotation requires specific environment variables to be set.

```{bash}
# Activate the environment containing Funannotate
ml funannotate

# Set the path to the Funannotate database.
export APPTAINERENV_FUNANNOTATE_DB=/90daydata/silage_microbiome/max_seq/batch1_all_barcodes/11_FunAnnotate/DB_FunannotateDatabase/funannotate_db

# Set the path for AUGUSTUS (a gene predictor) configuration files.
export AUGUSTUS_CONFIG_PATH=/90daydata/silage_microbiome/max_seq/batch1_all_barcodes/11_FunAnnotate/augustus/config
```


### Step 6.1: Create Repeat Library with Earl Grey

This step identifies transposable elements (TEs) to create a custom repeat library for soft masking. Soft masking changes the nucleotides of transposable elements and repeats to lower case so they are skipped by the annotation. Hard masking on the other hand deletes the repeated elements.

```{bash}
# Earl Grey is run via an apptainer (formerly singularity) container.
# Pull the container image first.
apptainer pull --disable-cache docker://tobybaril/earlgrey_dfam3.7:latest

# Run Earl Grey
# -g: Input genome FASTA.
# -s: Species name.
# -t: Number of threads.
# -o: Output directory.
apptainer run earlgrey_dfam3.7_latest.sif earlGrey -g polished56_assembly.fasta -s fc56 -t 20 -o TE_EarlGrey/

#for batch, make directory
sbatch -A silage_microbiome --array=36-45 -N 1 -n 16 --mem=50000 -p ceres -t 5:00:00 --wrap='in=${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}; mkdir -p 11_FunAnnotate/TE_EarlGrey/bar${in}; apptainer run earlgrey_dfam3.7_latest.sif earlGrey -g 07_Polished_Genome/cleaned_bar${in}_pol_assembly.fasta -s fus${in} -t 40 -o 11_FunAnnotate/TE_EarlGrey/bar${in}
```

### Step 6.2: Sort and Mask Assembly

Prepare the assembly by sorting contig and soft-masking the repeats identified by Earl Grey

```{bash}
# 1. Sort contigs by length and filter out short ones (minimum of 1kb in this case).
funannotate sort -i polished56_assembly.fasta --minlen 1000 -o bar56_assem_sort.fa

# 2. Soft-mask the assembly using the custom repeat library.
# -m repeatmodeler: Specifies the masking tool.
# -l: Path to the repeat library from Earl Grey (fc56-families.fa).
funannotate mask -i bar56_assem_sort.fa -m repeatmodeler -l TE_EarlGrey/fc56-families.fa -o assem_56_masked.fa

#batch sort and mask
sbatch -A silage_microbiome --array=37-45 -N 1 -n 2 --mem=5000 -p ceres -t 20:00 --wrap='in=${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}; funannotate sort -i 07_Polished_Genome/cleaned_bar${in}_pol_assembly.fasta --minlen 1000 -o 11a_FunAnnotateOut/bar${in}_assem_sort.fa && funannotate mask -i 11a_FunAnnotateOut/bar${in}_assem_sort.fa -m repeatmodeler -l 11_FunAnnotate/TE_EarlGrey/bar${in}/fus${in}_EarlGrey/fus${in}_Database/fus${in}-families.fa -o 11a_FunAnnotateOut/assem_${in}_masked.fa'
```

XXXSTOPXXX
### Step 6.3: Predict Genes

This is the core prediction step. It uses evidence from BUSCO, protein databases, and ab initio predictors like AUGUSTUS. `funannotate` $funannotate$ first trains its predictors on your specific genome.

```{bash}
# Run the main prediction pipeline.
# This is a complex command submitted as a batch job.
sbatch -N 1 -n 20 --mem=50000 -p short -q msn -t 2-0 --wrap="\
funannotate predict \
  -i assem_56_masked.fa \
  -s 'Fusarium sp. Bar56' \
  --protein_evidence ../BD_BuscoDatabase/sordariomycetes_odb10/refseq_db.faa \
  -o FunAnnotate_Bar56 \
  --cpu 20 \
  --busco_db sordariomycetes \
  --optimize_augustus"

# Explanation of key flags:
# -i: Input soft-masked assembly.
# -s: Species name (in quotes).
# --protein_evidence: Path to a FASTA file of related proteins (e.g., from UniProt or a BUSCO DB).
# -o: Output directory.
# --cpu: Number of CPUs.
# --busco_db: Ortholog database to guide prediction (e.g., sordariomycetes).
# --optimize_augustus: Tells funannotate to train AUGUSTUS, improving prediction accuracy.
```
